Formatted Simple Linear Regression and ANOVA Tables

Source: R/table_regression.R
table_regression.Rd

Fits a simple linear regression model and produces a publication-quality formatted table using the gt package. Two table types are available:

  • Regression Table (table_type = "regression", the default): Displays the intercept and slope with their standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, and a confidence interval for the slope. Includes an automatically generated plain-English interpretation of the confidence interval.

  • ANOVA Table (table_type = "anova"): Displays the analysis of variance decomposition of the regression model – Model (Regression), Error (Residuals), and Total rows – with sums of squares, degrees of freedom, mean squares, the F-statistic, and p-value. Includes \(R^2\) in the footer.

This function accepts input via formula and data only – raw data is required to fit the model.

Usage

table_regression(formula, data, table_type = "regression", conf_level = 0.95)

Arguments

formula

A two-sided formula of the form response ~ explanatory identifying the response (y) and explanatory (x) variables in your dataset. For example, formula = mpg ~ wt where both columns exist in data.

data

A data frame containing the variables named in formula.

table_type

A character string controlling which table is produced. Must be one of:

"regression"

(default) Produces a coefficients table with the intercept and slope, standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, and a confidence interval for the slope. A plain-English interpretation of the slope confidence interval is shown in the table footer.

"anova"

Produces an ANOVA decomposition table showing the Model (Regression), Error (Residuals), and Total rows with sums of squares, degrees of freedom, mean squares, the F-statistic, and p-value. \(R^2\) is shown in the table footer.

conf_level

The confidence level for the slope confidence interval shown in the regression table. Must be a number strictly between 0 and

  1. Default is 0.95 (95% confidence interval). Only used when table_type = "regression".

Value

A gt table object. The table can be further customized using standard gt functions, or saved to HTML, PDF, or Word using gt::gtsave().

Details

Regression Table

The regression table displays the fitted model $$\hat{y} = b_0 + b_1 x$$ with the following columns:

  • Term: Intercept (\(b_0\)) and the explanatory variable name (\(b_1\))

  • Coefficient: The estimated value of each term

  • SE: Standard error of the estimate

  • t-stat: The standardized test statistic (\(t = b_1 / SE_{b_1}\))

  • p-value: Two-sided p-value for testing H\(_0\): \(\beta = 0\)

  • Lower / Upper: Confidence interval bounds for each term

The footer provides a plain-English interpretation of the slope confidence interval, automatically adjusting wording based on whether the interval is entirely positive, entirely negative, or straddles zero.

ANOVA Table

The ANOVA table decomposes the total variability in the response variable into two sources:

  • Model (Regression): Variability explained by the linear relationship with the explanatory variable (\(SS_{Model}\), \(df = 1\))

  • Error (Residuals): Variability left unexplained by the model (\(SS_{Error}\), \(df = n - 2\))

  • Total: Total variability in the response (\(SS_{Total}\), \(df = n - 1\))

The F-statistic is \(F = MS_{Model} / MS_{Error}\) and tests the same null hypothesis as the slope t-test: H\(_0\): \(\beta_1 = 0\). \(R^2 = SS_{Model} / SS_{Total}\) is shown in the footer.

Connection Between the Two Tables

For simple linear regression with one explanatory variable, the F-test in the ANOVA table and the two-sided t-test for the slope in the regression table are mathematically equivalent: \(F = t^2\) and both produce the same p-value.

Examples

# --- Regression table, mtcars ---
# Does car weight predict fuel efficiency?
table_regression(
  formula = mpg ~ wt,
  data    = mtcars
)



  
Simple Linear Regression Output
    
Model: mpg ~ wt
    
Term
      Coefficient
      Std. Error
      t-statistic
      p-value
      Lower 95%
      Upper 95%
    
Intercept
37.2851
1.8776
19.8576
<1e-04
33.4505
41.1198
wt
−5.3445
0.5591
−9.5590
<1e-04
−6.4863
−4.2026
CI Interpretation: We are 95% confident that for every 1 unit increase in wt, the true population mean mpg will decrease between 4.2026 and 6.4863 units.
    

# --- Regression table, custom confidence level ---
table_regression(
  formula    = mpg ~ wt,
  data       = mtcars,
  conf_level = 0.99
)



  
Simple Linear Regression Output
    
Model: mpg ~ wt
    
Term
      Coefficient
      Std. Error
      t-statistic
      p-value
      Lower 99%
      Upper 99%
    
Intercept
37.2851
1.8776
19.8576
<1e-04
32.1217
42.4486
wt
−5.3445
0.5591
−9.5590
<1e-04
−6.8820
−3.8069
CI Interpretation: We are 99% confident that for every 1 unit increase in wt, the true population mean mpg will decrease between 3.8069 and 6.882 units.
    

# --- ANOVA table, mtcars ---
table_regression(
  formula    = mpg ~ wt,
  data       = mtcars,
  table_type = "anova"
)



  
ANOVA Table: Simple Linear Regression
    
Model: mpg ~ wt
    
Source
      Sum of Squares
      df
      Mean Square
      F-statistic
      p-value
    
Model (Regression)
847.7252
1
847.7252
91.3753
<1e-04
Error (Residuals)
278.3219
30
9.2774
–
–
Total
1,126.0472
31
–
–
–
R² = 0.7528 – The linear relationship with wt explains 75.28% of the variability in mpg.
    

# --- Regression table, ISI dataset ---
# Does study time predict GPA?
table_regression(
  formula = gpa ~ hours,
  data    = gpa
)



  
Simple Linear Regression Output
    
Model: gpa ~ hours
    
Term
      Coefficient
      Std. Error
      t-statistic
      p-value
      Lower 95%
      Upper 95%
    
Intercept
3.5977
0.1857
19.3702
<1e-04
3.2223
3.9731
hours
−0.0059
0.0031
−1.9166
0.0624
−0.0121
0.0003
CI Interpretation: We are 95% confident that for every 1 unit increase in hours, the true population mean gpa will change by between -0.0121 and 3e-04 units.
    

# --- ANOVA table, ISI dataset ---
table_regression(
  formula    = gpa ~ hours,
  data       = gpa,
  table_type = "anova"
)



  
ANOVA Table: Simple Linear Regression
    
Model: gpa ~ hours
    
Source
      Sum of Squares
      df
      Mean Square
      F-statistic
      p-value
    
Model (Regression)
0.6803
1
0.6803
3.6735
0.0624
Error (Residuals)
7.4079
40
0.1852
–
–
Total
8.0882
41
–
–
–
R² = 0.0841 – The linear relationship with hours explains 8.41% of the variability in gpa.
    

# --- Regression table, iris dataset ---
# Does sepal length predict petal length?
table_regression(
  formula = Petal.Length ~ Sepal.Length,
  data    = iris
)



  
Simple Linear Regression Output
    
Model: Petal.Length ~ Sepal.Length
    
Term
      Coefficient
      Std. Error
      t-statistic
      p-value
      Lower 95%
      Upper 95%
    
Intercept
−7.1014
0.5067
−14.0161
<1e-04
−8.1027
−6.1002
Sepal.Length
1.8584
0.0859
21.6460
<1e-04
1.6888
2.0281
CI Interpretation: We are 95% confident that for every 1 unit increase in Sepal.Length, the true population mean Petal.Length will increase between 1.6888 and 2.0281 units.
    

# --- ANOVA table, iris dataset ---
table_regression(
  formula    = Petal.Length ~ Sepal.Length,
  data       = iris,
  table_type = "anova"
)



  
ANOVA Table: Simple Linear Regression
    
Model: Petal.Length ~ Sepal.Length
    
Source
      Sum of Squares
      df
      Mean Square
      F-statistic
      p-value
    
Model (Regression)
352.8662
1
352.8662
468.5502
<1e-04
Error (Residuals)
111.4592
148
0.7531
–
–
Total
464.3254
149
–
–
–
R² = 0.76 – The linear relationship with Sepal.Length explains 76% of the variability in Petal.Length.
